Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty chaotic. I'd try to set aside time during the week, but with everything going on, I usually ended up cramming when I absolutely had to. I'm a visual learner, so I'd spend a lot of time making diagrams and color-coded notes, but that happened in bursts rather than consistently. I aimed for about 15 hours a week, but those hours were scattered all over the place depending on my anxiety levels and what else was demanding my attention.
Q2: My pattern varied wildly depending on deadlines and how overwhelmed I was feeling. Some weeks I'd barely touch anything, and then I'd have these intense periods where I'd work through the night. The regularity I wanted just never materialized because my stress levels would spike and I'd freeze up, then have to catch up later. It was exhausting, honestly, but I somehow made it work in the end.
Q3: Usually it was panic, if I'm being honest. I'd realize a deadline was approaching or I'd get a notification about something due, and that fear would push me to log in. Sometimes the gamified tasks helped motivate me because they felt less intimidating than regular assignments. The little achievements and progress bars gave me something concrete to focus on when everything else felt overwhelming.
Q4: There were brief periods where logging in became more routine, especially when I got into the rhythm of the gamified elements. Seeing my progress visualized made me want to keep the streak going. But that routine would break whenever my anxiety got really bad or life got complicated. I'd disappear for a while and then have to rebuild that habit from scratch, which was frustrating.
Q5: The video content was incredibly helpful for me because I'm such a visual learner. I watched over 100 minutes of video material because seeing concepts demonstrated stuck with me way better than reading. The gamified tasks were also useful because they broke things down into manageable chunks that didn't feel as scary. When I could see my progress visually, it helped calm my anxiety a bit.
Q6: I pretty much ignored the discussion forums except for posting twice. They made me anxious because I was scared of saying something wrong or looking stupid in front of other students. I also delayed a lot of the regular assignments, which is why my completion rate was so low. The fear of not doing them perfectly kept me from starting them at all sometimes.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time when I could see immediate results or progress, like with the gamified elements. If I could visualize what I was learning or see a clear connection to the exam, I'd engage with it. But if something felt abstract or the payoff seemed too distant, my anxiety would convince me it wasn't worth the stress. I needed that instant feedback loop to keep me going.
Q8: Optional activities felt like a trap, honestly. Part of me knew they'd help, but the fact that they weren't required made it easier for my anxious brain to skip them. Required activities came with the fear of failure attached, which paradoxically sometimes made me avoid them too, but at least I knew I had to face them eventually. The gamified optional stuff was different though—it felt lower stakes and actually kind of fun.
Q9: Absolutely. I downloaded a lot of materials and worked with them offline where I felt less pressure. I'd print out diagrams and annotate them by hand, or create visual study guides that I'd review away from my computer. I also used some external YouTube videos and resources when the course materials weren't clicking for me. A lot of my actual learning happened with my notes spread out on my desk, not in front of the LMS.
Q10: There were entire study sessions where I'd be working intensely with printed materials or my hand-drawn diagrams, and the LMS would show nothing. I'd sometimes watch a video once, take detailed notes, and then study those notes for hours offline. My assignment scores were actually pretty good when I submitted, which shows I was learning, but that learning wasn't happening in trackable ways.
Q11: Yes, constantly. I'd download a PDF or watch a video once, then work with that material extensively offline. It was actually better for my anxiety to study away from the computer because the LMS interface reminded me of all the things I hadn't done yet. Having physical materials felt more manageable and less overwhelming than being logged into a system tracking my every move.
Q12: The hardest times were when my anxiety peaked, which happened a lot this semester. I'd look at everything I needed to do and just freeze completely. There were weeks where I couldn't bring myself to log in at all because the fear of seeing how behind I was felt unbearable. My attendance dropped to around 30% during those periods, and I'm honestly scared about how close I came to failing.
Q13: My anxiety was the biggest factor affecting everything. When stress hit hard, I'd avoid the LMS entirely because it represented all my failures and fears. Fatigue from sleepless anxious nights made it harder to focus when I did engage. I had the resources and tools I needed, but my mental state was the real barrier. Competing responsibilities gave me excuses to avoid facing the course when I was scared.
Q14: Definitely. Sometimes I'd log in and just stare at the screen, too anxious to actually process anything. I'd click through materials without really absorbing them, just going through the motions to feel like I was doing something. Those sessions were more about managing my guilt than actual learning. The engagement was surface-level at best during those times.
Q15: I postponed assignments constantly, even when I knew they were important. The fear of not doing them perfectly paralyzed me. I'd tell myself I'd start tomorrow when I felt better, but that better day rarely came until the deadline was breathing down my neck. It's a terrible cycle—the more I postponed, the more anxious I got, which made it even harder to start.
Q16: Deadlines were both my enemy and my savior. They caused intense spikes in my anxiety, but they were also the only thing that could break through my avoidance. I'd do most of my LMS activity in the days right before something was due. Without that external pressure, I'd float in this anxious limbo of knowing I should work but being too scared to start.
Q17: When I got good feedback on assignments, it helped a lot with my confidence and made me more likely to engage. But negative or critical feedback could send me spiraling and make me avoid the course for days. The gamified feedback was easier to handle because it felt less personal—just points and progress bars rather than someone judging my work directly.
Q18: The course structure was honestly overwhelming at times. Having eight online courses meant there was always something demanding attention, which fed my anxiety. The gamified elements helped by making some tasks feel more approachable and less scary. But the overall structure of deadlines and assignments felt relentless, and I struggled to keep up with only 44% task completion.
Q19: The gamified tasks made engagement easier because they felt like smaller, achievable goals rather than massive scary assignments. The visual progress tracking helped my visual learning style and gave me concrete evidence I was moving forward. What made it harder was the sheer volume of content and the traditional assignment structure that triggered my perfectionism and fear of failure.
Q20: I think that describes me somewhat. I was logged in frequently and watched a lot of videos, but my assignment completion was really low. For me, it was anxiety—I was engaging with the learning materials but too scared to submit work that might not be perfect. Sometimes exploring and learning feels safer than putting yourself out there with actual submissions that get judged.
Q21: That's partially my story too, in a way. My exam score was decent despite low attendance and completion rates because I was learning effectively offline. When I actually sat down to study with my visual notes and diagrams, I could absorb material well. The LMS activity doesn't capture those hours of offline study that actually prepared me for the exam.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns of access and completion, which gives some picture of engagement. But they completely miss the quality of that engagement and all the learning happening offline. They can't capture mental state—whether someone is genuinely absorbing material or just clicking through in a panic. They also miss the fear and anxiety that might be preventing someone from engaging even when they want to.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material, not just going through the motions. For me, it's when I'm making those visual connections and the concepts click into place. It's wanting to learn even when it's hard, and pushing through the fear to keep trying. True engagement is mental and emotional investment, not just logging hours on a platform.
Q24: The LMS can't show how much I was fighting my own anxiety just to show up at all. It can't capture the hours I spent with printed notes and hand-drawn diagrams, genuinely learning. It doesn't reflect how the gamified elements actually helped me feel less scared, or how a good exam score came from intense offline preparation. Most importantly, it can't show that despite being terrified most of the semester, I kept climbing back and ultimately passed.
